one_line_takeaway: Membership inference cannot prove a production model trained on your data because you can never bound its false-positive rate without sampling the null hypothesis; sound proofs instead require pre-planted random canaries, watermarks, or verbatim data extraction, all of which reduce to the bound FPR <= k/|X|.
key_finding: The central claim: for large-scale production models it is impossible to bound an MI attack's false positive rate, because you cannot sample from the null hypothesis (you don't know the training set and cannot afford to retrain). Therefore every surveyed heuristic for estimating FPR is statistically unsound and gives model developers plausible deniability — this holds for current AND hypothetical future MI attacks. Two constructive positives: (1) the rank-based test yields the clean bound FPR <= k/|X| whenever the target is sampled uniformly at random and independently of the model; (2) three 'folklore' methods satisfy that condition and are sound — injecting random canaries (e.g. a hidden HTML string), watermarking training data (statistical test with interpretable p-values), and verbatim data extraction (finding a short/random prompt p such that f(p)=x, with FPR approximately <= |P|/|U|). A notable original sub-result is that dataset inference is unsound even with held-out IID drafts, because publishing x causally alters other training data.
core_contribution: This is a position/theory paper (Zhang, Das, Kamath, Tramer; ETH Zurich / Waterloo) arguing that membership inference (MI) attacks cannot serve as sound 'training data proofs' — evidence to a third party (e.g. a judge in a copyright lawsuit) that a production foundation model was trained on someone's data. Its own contributions are analytical, not experimental: (1) it formalizes a training data proof as a hypothesis test with an explicit null hypothesis H0 ('the model was trained on D0 = D without target x') vs H1 (trained with x), making precise a notion usually left implicit; (2) it argues that bounding the attack's false positive rate (FPR = Pr over training runs that T(f,x) lands in the rejection region under H0) is IMPOSSIBLE for large proprietary models, because one cannot sample from the null hypothesis (the training set D is unknown and retraining a foundation model is prohibitively expensive); (3) it introduces a rank-based hypothesis test and proves Lemma 1 (FPR <= k/|X| when the target is sampled uniformly and independently of the model), and uses this lens to show WHY three surveyed classes of prior 'sound-FPR' proposals fail; (4) it contributes an original causal critique of dataset inference — the mere act of publishing target data x can causally influence OTHER training samples (Wikipedia pages, fan content, news about x), so a model can rank x's real text above counterfactual drafts even when never trained on x. It then formalizes three paths that ARE sound under stated assumptions (random canaries, watermarking, verbatim data extraction), reframing all three as instances of its rank-based test.
threat_model: Adversarial setting is a 'training data proof': a data creator/owner C wants to convince a third party (judge) that model developer M trained model f on C's data x. C runs an MI attack and must demonstrate statistical soundness — specifically a bounded false positive rate, i.e. a low probability of wrongly accusing M. The adversary of interest is really the unsoundness/plausible-deniability afforded to M: because C cannot sample the null hypothesis (retrain f without x), M can always argue the observed model behavior on x is coincidental or caused by publication side-effects rather than by training on x. Attacker capabilities assumed for the MI attack are black-box query access to f and a loss-based test statistic (global threshold or likelihood-ratio). This is squarely an ML-privacy / security paper (membership inference, memorization, watermarking, data extraction).
limitations: By the authors' own framing this is a position paper: it contains no new empirical experiments, no trained models, and no benchmark tables — the quantitative figures it cites (watermark p-value, 5% watermark threshold) are borrowed from prior work as illustrations, not measured here. The three 'sound' paradigms are proven sound only under idealized assumptions that may be hard to meet in practice: canaries require the data owner to inject content BEFORE release (no help for already-published legacy works like existing books/articles at issue in the actual lawsuits that motivate the paper), the sizes of P and U in the data-extraction bound FPR <= |P|/|U| cannot be exactly characterized, and the 'strings in U are a priori equally likely' assumption is acknowledged as simplifying. The causal-publishing critique is argued via a fictional Harry Potter thought experiment rather than measured. Thus the constructive contribution is a framework and existence argument, not a deployable, evaluated tool.

datasets_used: No datasets are used for experiments; this is a theory/position paper. Datasets appear only as illustrative references: CIFAR-10 (canonical 'in-the-lab' MI setting), Pile / Pythia models (cited as a case where IID train-test splits are known), and arXiv papers around the OpenLLaMA Feb-2023 cutoff (from Meeus et al., discussed as a case study, not run by the authors).
quantitative_results: Lemma 1 (the paper's own result): for a rank-based test that flags membership when rank(f,x,X) <= k with x sampled uniformly and independently, FPR <= k/|X| (e.g. to achieve FPR <= 1%, the true canary must rank in the top 1% of the candidate set X).

Data-extraction bound (the paper's own formalization): FPR is approximately <= |P|/|U|, where P is the prompt set and U the universe of plausible outputs; short/random prompts minimize |P| and long high-entropy targets maximize |U|.

'Bad model' toy example (the paper's own): a construction where the true FPR is 1/2 but the naive a-posteriori estimate of Eq. 7 yields 0, showing the estimator can be arbitrarily wrong; Appendix A re-derives FPR = k/|X| for the rank test in this case.
Watermark illustration (numbers cited from Kirchenbauer et al. [22], not measured here): a passage with 28 green tokens where ~9 would be expected by chance corresponds to a p-value of ~7x10^-14.
Watermark detectability (cited from [34], not measured here): a model's exposure to watermarked data can be detected with low FPR even when as little as 5% of the training text is watermarked.

Data-extraction heuristics (cited from [6,30]): target strings should be ~50 tokens (2-3 sentences) and high-entropy, with short/random prompts; prior extraction work following these heuristics reported no observed false positives.
baselines_compared: No empirical baseline comparison is performed. The paper instead critiques prior training-data-proof / MI methods analytically: Shi et al. 'Detecting pretraining data' / Min-K% [35]; Min-K%++ [44]; Meeus et al. document-level MI 'Did the neurons read your book?' [27] and their debiased post-hoc / 'inherent challenges' work [26]; Nob-MIAs debiased construction [17]; DE-COP [16]; Meeus et al. 'copyright traps' [28]; and Maini et al. dataset inference [24,25] (the target of the original causal-publishing critique). It builds on Das et al. 'blind baselines beat MIA' [13] and Duan et al. 'Do MIAs work on LLMs?' [15] to argue distribution shifts make a-posteriori non-member collection unreliable.
